title: Nearly Isotropic Magnon Transport in Epitaxial Lithium Aluminum Ferrite Thin Films
authors: Yiming Li, Katya Mikhailova, Lerato Takana, Daisy O'Mahoney, Sauviz P. Alaei, Guanxiong Qu, Dominic Petruzzi, Samuel Crossley, Harold Y. Hwang, Ian R. Fisher, Clare C. Yu, Yuri Suzuki
abstract: Low-loss magnetic insulating thin films are promising for information transport via magnons, where isotropic in-plane magnon propagation is desirable. We report nonlocal measurements of electrically and thermally generated magnons in epitaxial (001) lithium aluminum ferrite Li$_{0.5}$Al$_{0.7}$Fe$_{1.8}$O$_4$ thin films with pronounced fourfold in-plane magnetic anisotropy. By measuring the inverse spin Hall signal as a function of the magnon diffusion distance, we deduce magnon diffusion lengths that are nearly identical along the [100] and [110] directions at 250~K. This isotropy is consistent with a nearly isotropic exchange stiffness. These results highlight spinel ferrites as viable platforms for isotropic magnon transport.
summary: This paper investigates magnon transport in lithium aluminum ferrite (LAFO) thin films to address the need for low-loss, isotropic magnetic waveguides. While previous spinel ferrites showed anisotropic transport, LAFO demonstrates nearly identical magnon diffusion lengths along different in-plane directions. This isotropy is attributed to isotropic exchange stiffness and potential angular averaging. These findings suggest that LAFO is a promising platform for developing efficient, isotropic magnon-based information interconnects.
main_problem: The study aims to determine if the anisotropic magnon transport observed in magnesium aluminum ferrite (MAFO) is a general feature of spinel ferrites or if isotropic in-plane magnon propagation can be achieved for information transport.
main_result: The researchers found that epitaxial lithium aluminum ferrite (LAFO) thin films exhibit nearly isotropic magnon diffusion lengths along the [100] and [110] directions at 250 K, despite having pronounced fourfold in-plane magnetic anisotropy.
method: Nonlocal transport measurements using platinum electrodes were used to detect electrically (Spin Hall Effect) and thermally (Spin Seebeck Effect) generated magnons, combined with SQUID magnetometry and broadband Ferromagnetic Resonance (FMR).
model_or_system: The physical system consists of epitaxial (001) lithium aluminum ferrite (LAFO) thin films grown on MgAl2O4 substrates, characterized by a spinel structure with significant in-plane magnetic anisotropy.
key_observables: Magnon diffusion length (lambda), nonlocal resistance (R_SHE and R_SSE), saturation magnetization (Ms), anisotropy field (H4,parallel), and Gilbert damping (alpha).
important_parameters: Temperature of 250 K, magnetic field of 300 mT, and film thickness of approximately 12 nm.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a 1D magnon diffusion model and attributes the observed isotropy to nearly isotropic exchange stiffness and effective angular averaging due to inhomogeneous broadening.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows magnetization field dependence and hysteresis loops; Fig 2 shows FMR frequency dependence; Fig 3 demonstrates the angular dependence of nonlocal resistance (cos^2 and cos dependence).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnon anisotropy, describes the growth and structural characterization of LAFO films, presents magnetic property measurements (SQUID and FMR), details the nonlocal transport experiments, and concludes with a discussion on the mechanism of isotropic transport.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. (a) Field dependence of the FMR signal at 5 GHz and 250 K. (b) Frequency dependence of HFMR at 250 K can be fit to the Kittel equation to deduce the Land´e g-factor, effective magnetization Meff and in-plane anisotropy field H4,∥along the [100] and [110] axes.

FIG. 1. Field dependence of the magnetization for a (001) LAFO film on (001) MAO along the in-plane [100] and [110] directions at 250 K. (inset) Schematic diagram of the LAFO film showing the [001] out-of-plane axis and the [100] and [110] in-plane axes.

FIG. 4. Amplitudes of the SHE nonlocal resistance ∆RSHE (upper panel) and amplitudes of the SSE nonlocal resistance ∆RSSE (lower panel) as a function of the magnon propaga- tion distance d along the [100] and [110] directions at 250 K. Measurements were taken with an injection charge current of I0 = 0.4 mA, 0.5 mA and 0.6 mA on each device and one or two devices at the same distance d. A single best-fit curve is plotted for each orientation and for each type of nonlocal resistance.

FIG. 3. Nonlocal spin transport measurements along the [100] directions show (a) SHE nonlocal resistance RSHE and (b) SSE nonlocal resistance RSSE as a function of the angle γ between the magnetization and the magnon propagation di- rection. Similar SHE and SSE measurements are shown for magnon propagation along the [110] direction in (c) and (d). These measurements were taken on a device with Pt elec- trodes a distance d = 0.5 µm apart at 250 K and 300 mT with 3 different injection currents I0: 0.4 mA (yellow), 0.5 mA (grey) and 0.6 mA (blue).
